source_title: Digging Deeper: A Canadian Reporter's Research Guide
author: Robert Cribb, Dean Jobb, David McKie, Fred Vallance-Jones

extracts.extract: Court records are an unsurpassed resource for compiling background information about a person. Has the person sued or been sued? Has he or she broken the law and, if so, what was the outcome? Who are the person's associates and business partners, past and present? Does the person own property or operate a business that has become mired in a legal dispute? Check out court records wherever the person has lived. To get started, do a keyword search for the person's name in the Canadian Legal Information Institute's online database of court rulings.
extracts.notes: Canadian-specific CANLII resource; principle is universal

extracts.extract: Are government agencies and inspectors doing their jobs? To find out, dig into the files of prosecutions for violations of the laws and regulations that govern workplace and consumer safety, the environment, labour standards, and other priorities. They will disclose if people and business are being held accountable and whether regulators are being vigilant in protecting the public interest.

Are courts being run efficiently? Are judges handing down sentences that are too soft or too harsh? Are prosecutors going easy on offenders who are wealthy or powerful? Do the authorities deal differently with members of visible minorities? The paperwork of the justice system is the key to compiling evidence, statistics, sources, and case studies the investigative reporter needs to answer these and other important questions, and to help ensure justice is done.
It is often easier to obtain historical records than more recent ones, which may be in private hands or subject to access restrictions.

Accuracy can be a concern—content is posted and revised by users who may not be experts on the subject—but footnotes and hyperlinks allow researchers to quickly identify and locate books, articles, websites, and other sources that provide more reliable and detailed information.
extracts.notes: About Wikipedia specifically but principle applies broadly to user-generated content

extracts.extract: If no index is available, you will have to search based on knowing the approximate date a story appeared or an event occurred. To locate newspaper stories about an old murder case, for example, look up the court file at the archives, find out when each hearing was held, and then consult the following day's newspapers to see what was reported.

Google, through Google Books, offers access to a vast and expanding full-text online database that includes numerous Canadian titles searchable by keyword. Older works that are in the public domain can be read online. All books in the database can be searched for free regardless of copyright, so it can be used to identify books worth purchasing or borrowing from a library. Some of these books may not have an index, making this a valuable tool for finding references that would otherwise remain hidden or require extensive research to locate.

Many public libraries maintain vertical files—a collection of indexed folders containing material on a wide range of subjects. They are a key source of information about local history, prominent people who have lived or made their mark in the area, and local, regional, or national issues of importance to the community. A typical vertical file contains a hodgepodge of information—newspaper clippings, magazine articles, pamphlets, reports—all of it compiled over the years and much of it difficult, if not impossible, to find anywhere else.

Think of archives as the attics of the nation. They preserve government documents, yellowed newspapers, older court files and property records, photos, film and sound recordings, genealogical records, and the diaries and personal papers of famous and ordinary Canadians alike.

Journalists may find allies among archives staff as they try to hunt down information. While researching his book on the Steven Truscott case, Julian Sher recalls, he gave up on efforts to locate the military's personnel file of a suspect the police overlooked in the rush to convict Truscott. But one archivist refused to accept defeat and eventually found the file, which contained hundreds of pages of material and provided details of the man's mental health and his whereabouts at the time of the murder.

Keep in mind that many people have a personal archive of old letters, newspaper clippings, family photos, and other keepsakes. 'I ask people if they have anything—letters, papers, photographs—that might shed light on the story I'm working on or might corroborate things they're telling me,' says author and journalist Stephen Kimber.

Whatever story you're working on, you need to find out what kind of paperwork has to get filed and with whom. Whether it's real estate transactions, police investigations, election campaigns, or divorces, just about anything we do of note generates official records somewhere. Quite often these documents are in the public domain. The key is find out what and where they are and figure out how to get them.

There are some basic questions to ask yourself before you begin the process of building a profile on an individual. What is their background? Have they been in the public spotlight? What has been written about them already? What is their relationship to others who might be relevant to the broader story you're researching? Where does this person fit in the larger context of the story? Who are the people around your subject who might be valuable sources of information? What does your subject do for a living? Is he or she a member of a regulated or licensed profession? Have they had brushes with the law that would have produced court records? What has your subject written that might be publicly available?

Get a file folder with lots of separate pockets, and use them. As you work through the various methods outlined here, you will accumulate records. It's essential that all the paper you gather be kept organized. If you're working on an in-depth project, you'll likely end up with stacks of pages of material whose meaning will become confusing days, weeks, or months later when it comes time to structure and write. Even worse, essential things will get lost. When you come across a valuable website or online article, print it and add it to an electronic folder in your computer. You'll want backups. And keep plenty of written notes on your material to remind you later of what is important. It's also worth creating an electronic spreadsheet file in which you log your phone calls, interviews, documents, and notes. Another useful file is a chronology of your subject's involvement in your story, allowing you to track the evolution of a story over time in a way that your brain simply won't be able to.
Recognize that the more organized and methodical you are in building your file of material, the more useful it will prove to be when you begin to write.

The Internet doesn't make a stupid journalist smart, but it does make a smart journalist smarter. A lot of people just use it as a blind search engine and they don't even use it that well. But if you use it as a finely honed tool, it can be very, very effective.

General search bars are for amateur researchers. They exist for teens looking for fast access to the latest music video. Not you. Experienced Web searchers need to be more precise, using advanced search options that filter out much of the irrelevant material. All of the major search engines provide instructions for navigating their particular advanced search tools. It's worth learning the skills for several of them. No single search engine covers the whole Internet; most search only a small portion. The more engines you use, the broader your coverage.

The most basic advice I can give for effective Web searching is to be patient. Don't expect a search engine to give you the answers you need in its first few results. While Google's algorithm ranks results by importance, it's quite possible that your dream document doesn't fit into that formula. Go beyond the first few pages of results, and click on anything that looks useful. The answer you need may be buried on the 10th page of results.
extracts.notes: Quote from Astrid Lange, head librarian at the Toronto Star

extracts.extract: Also be aware that search engines like Google and Bing customize your results based on your search history (known as filter bubbles). To avoid this, clear your cookies and cache regularly, or try a search engine like DuckDuckGo which avoids filter bubbles.

The first step is to do some thinking about the question you're asking. Computers are literal. They have no ability to interpret a clumsy array of keywords. It's useful to imagine the ideal document and the words it would contain. Remember that concepts can be expressed in a number of ways; your coming up with an exhaustive list of keywords will help you to unearth documents that would have never turned up in more superficial searches.

It's a way to find someone really specific. If you put orphans, and Africa into a search engine, you'll get everybody. But if you ask for a slide show—who has bothered putting together a slide show and putting it up on a webpage? It's got to be an expert.
extracts.notes: Quote from Julian Sher; refers to filetype: search operator

extracts.extract: The best stories always pull the camera lens back on an issue's big picture. The forest gives the tree context. That's where subject directories come in. These tools give you the kind of fast overview that will give you ideas, resources, and confidence that you're telling a tale that fits within a larger narrative. They're authoritative, often curated, built on a public good instead of a profit model, and are sufficiently broad in scope to serve as a strong starting point for the in-depth research that you won't find by typing a few words into a search engine.

If the site you're looking for has disappeared, it may not be gone forever. The online archiving tool at www.archive.org can help you access websites that have been pulled down. Because it's easy to remove a website from the Internet instantly, it's always a good idea to save a copy of a site onto your hard drive if you know it's going to be important to your research. In some cases, people will pull their sites down once they get a sense that authorities or journalists are looking into their affairs. If you haven't saved a copy, it may be difficult to prove what had been posted previously. The best method here is doing a screen grab of the site by saving an image of what is on screen to your hard drive.

Setting up Internet news alerts is one of the first things Julian Sher does when he begins an investigation. At any given time, he's got at least three or four alerts delivering stories to his email every day. 'If you're working on a long project you want to be able to follow it as it evolves. It gives you leads you wouldn't have thought of because names of people and experts come up.'

There's another kind of expert that journalists often fail to consider. Activists can be incredibly useful sources despite their obvious biases. Using the Internet to get their message out, they provide journalists with gushers of studies, documents, and networks of human sources that could otherwise take weeks to collect. As long as you remain aware of an activist's partiality, the information can still prove useful. Many activists or advocacy groups also collect official records such as government studies and reports, along with newspaper clippings, which come from legitimate and credible sources.

Social media has changed the way we do research because sites like Facebook, Twitter, and LinkedIn allow us to find information about people that we previously could not find. We used to be limited to searching news or legal databases or just the general web to put together a profile on an individual. If a person had not been involved in anything of note, this type of search might not reveal much. But now people post a variety of information on social media allowing us to put together a more robust profile.

Privacy settings are often in place to block access to pages of newsmakers, but the search shouldn't stop when a privacy wall appears. Typically, a friends list is accessible, thus providing an instant source list that can be cross-referenced. Clicking on each of those friend pages offers the possibility of some being accessible with comments, shared photos, and insights.

Before you're writing a single word about anyone, you should know everything that has already been written. That means a thorough search of media databases and the Internet. Study previous stories carefully, make notes, and keep the material organized as you work through your story. Look for the unanswered questions in those stories, issues that need to be followed, and names of sources who might be able to help you.

It's also important not to limit yourself too much in terms of the scope of your reading. In the process of producing a documentary and a book on the David Milgaard story in the early 1990s, CBC journalist Cecil Rosner read a year's worth of Saskatoon's The StarPhoenix newspapers prior to the Gail Miller murder. This provided valuable context about the nature of the community, the attitudes to hippies and counter-culture which played a role in police activities, the nature of the police force itself, the attitudes of the police chief, etc. It gave me information I would have never thought to ask about.

Searching media databases can be daunting. Putting a couple of keywords into a database can turn up thousands of hits. So you'll need to develop techniques for limiting such searches to find the major pieces you need to know. Consider limiting the results to stories that are 1000 words or over; this filter will remove small news stories, and focus your search on more in-depth pieces.

Also, consider the less-obvious information published in the media, such as obituaries. Julian Sher recalls the challenge of finding the family of a dead man from Halifax with a common surname. 'We did the standard directory searches in Halifax, then moved outward to Nova Scotia and then the Maritimes. My editor suggested quietly that we do a special search for the obit. Sure enough, in the obit, they mentioned all the next of kin and then we found the mother.'

Be sure to save or copy important stories from media databases, as you will likely need to consult them later. Mark them up. Highlight key facts. Put question marks around issues you need to explore. Circle names of people you'll want to talk to. Spending a few hours—or days—just reading is likely the best way to get your brain thinking critically about how to proceed.

Resumés are the calling cards of the working world. They also document how people choose to represent themselves to potential employers. This is an undervalued resource. It provides a road map of a person's life and career, and is invaluable in any investigation. It is often filed with public applications, and therefore is often publicly available.
In general, it's possible to build a road map of any person's life by thoroughly analyzing public records.

As with other documents, don't assume the information contained in a resumé is true. You should report only information you have verified. Resumés can open up research possibilities, however, and often mention references who can be added to your interview list.

I'm always amazed at how many professions are regulated either by their self-governing bodies or government—investment advisors, funeral directors, architects, dieticians, veterinarians. All accumulate information about members of their profession that you're entitled to see or at least know about. Regulation means the possibility of open and public sanction, and that's gold.

After building a database containing every disciplinary decision by the Ontario College of Physicians and Surgeons over a six-year period, the journalists noticed how Bradley's name was among a number of physicians with a troubled medical history in the province. This led them to uncover more details of Bradley's long trail of medical negligence. A remarkable story began to unfold from the records of medical regulatory bodies in three different jurisdictions.
extracts.notes: Toronto Star investigation into Dr. Kenneth Bradley

extracts.extract: Never underestimate the power of a solid paper trail. Even in a story rich with characters and details, documents can play an important role—not just in buttressing your story with key facts (and thus providing some legal protection)—but also by providing some key story-telling nuggets and gems.

Always try to get hold of the main search police warrant in the case you're investigating. Don't let court officials bully you by insisting you can't have them. Unless they are sealed by court order, you have a right to a copy. Search warrants are a journalist's gold mine. For one thing, they provide a certain legal privilege or immunity because you can quote them to explain what the police are alleging without having to necessarily prove the truthfulness of the allegations. And because police also often meticulously lay out their case, search warrants also become powerful storytelling devices.

Following the money is always a core mission in the backgrounding of individuals and companies. And that pursuit is often intricately connected to liens and property records. The supplementary information provided by lien records can be as useful as the main details of a person's loan. For instance, you might find the subject's date of birth, address, vehicle make and model, and vehicle identification number, allowing you to do a vehicle record search.

One key trick here was knowing to search, not just for the names of the Ford family members, but for the corporate entities. If we hadn't searched for Deco or Doug Ford Holdings Inc., we never would have found the commercial parcels of land, which are the family's most valuable properties.
extracts.notes: Quote from Greg McArthur, Globe and Mail

extracts.extract: I use land title searches and lien searches all the time, but it's usually for a much simpler purpose—locating people, and their home address, so I can seek them out and interview them. But this research was a great reminder that the documents are so much more important. These sorts of records, and the fine print in those records, often tell a story—and in this case, it was a story that clearly contradicted the narrative being promoted by Toronto's chief magistrate.

A name, for example, might prompt a lien search that reveals a home address and business address. A reverse search on the business address might provide the name of a company. The name of the company might prompt a corporate search that turns up other officers and directors who are business partners of the person you're researching. A lien search on the company might provide still more details such as the company's level of debt and list of creditors—more potential sources of information. It might also provide information on property or vehicles that can lead to other material from motor vehicle searches and land-title inquiries.

Whatever your searches turn up, it's important to go back and read documents again, days or weeks after you obtain them. Names, addresses, or company numbers that meant little to you when you first encountered them may suddenly ring bells when you examine them later with the benefit of further research.

The most important thing is to develop a mindset that prompts you to consider all of the potential research possibilities, to find alternative ways of getting information when roadblocks appear, and always to keep digging.

Analysis of 563 Taser firings indicated three-quarters of suspects were unarmed. The data also documented a host of related injuries—including head wounds, burns, and lacerations—linked to a weapon police have touted as less harmful than pepper spray or batons. The figures also revealed a disturbing tendency to painfully jolt suspects after only the most cursory interaction. Far from being a tool to defuse major conflicts that might otherwise have required lethal force, officers repeatedly used the Taser as a compliance tool.

It took access-to-information requests and six months of repeated calls to the RCMP to learn that little had been done to verify the electrical output of Tasers. Furthermore, in analyzing the research material the police force used in making decisions, we realized the RCMP had not only relied on some seriously flawed studies, but had accepted information from Taser International at face value.

Several experts analyzed the studies and concluded that they were either extremely limited or lacked scientific rigour. We also obtained a test protocol drafted by Taser International, which was valuable in designing our tests. We hired National Technical Systems, an internationally accredited engineering and testing firm, to randomly test more than 40 units of the most popular model. The results were troubling: More than 10 per cent of the guns were either defective or significantly off specifications.
extracts.notes: CP-CBC Taser investigation; commissioned independent lab testing

extracts.extract: The investigative team spent weeks compiling the best picture possible of RCMP Taser use, painstakingly typing numerous incident details from thousands of individual forms into the computer database. This groundbreaking analysis, illustrated with vivid details from telling cases, revealed that RCMP use of the controversial stun guns had spiked to 1000 incidents annually from just 600 in 2005.
extracts.notes: CP-CBC Taser investigation; manual data entry from forms into database

extracts.extract: It allowed us to move beyond the anecdotal evidence to state with a great degree of certainty what was happening. It is a reminder to me that while hunches and isolated reports are valuable, there is no substitute for years' worth of data that paints a reliable picture.

No matter how good you get at digging through documents, trolling databases, and unearthing archival material, a thorough investigation must involve talking to people. You begin dealing with people from the very beginning of a project: experts, tipsters, government bureaucrats, clerks, secretaries, accident witnesses, and so on. In some cases, when not many documents are available, human sources may give you much of your story.
extracts.chapter: Getting People to Talk: The Art of the Interview

extracts.extract: An interview is not much like ordinary conversation at all. An interview is actually a unique verbal form. Mastering the form takes hard work, a dose of psychology, and a willingness to learn from mistakes. An interview is characterized by a delicate balance of power. The interviewer controls the topics; the interviewee controls the information. Conducting an interview effectively is a kind of verbal seduction. A big part of your job is to raise the comfort level of your subject so he or she will want to talk.
extracts.chapter: Getting People to Talk: The Art of the Interview

extracts.extract: A way to get reluctant sources to talk is to be armed with information. The corporate CEO is more likely to speak to you if you already have the guts of the story. It won't be possible for the company to halt your investigation merely by not speaking with you. In fact, you can advance the legitimate argument that it will be in the subject's best interest to speak to you to ensure that the firm's point of view is heard.
extracts.chapter: Getting People to Talk: The Art of the Interview

extracts.extract: Especially when a story contains findings that will cast an organization or person(s) in a poor light, it is important that you make every effort to obtain an interview or statement. If you get nowhere with telephone calls, and emails or direct messages are ignored, you may need to send a letter—best by registered mail because this assures receipt is documented—repeating the request for an interview and outlining the areas you wish to cover in the interview, as well as key allegations or facts to which you are seeking a response.
extracts.chapter: Getting People to Talk: The Art of the Interview

extracts.extract: Some interviews may be only or mainly information (witnesses to a fire or car crash, for example), some might be emotion (a tearful family reunion with a returning soldier or hostage), and others might be accountability (a government minister). The best interviews have all three. And good interviews proceed in that order from information to emotion to accountability. It's pretty hard to start with a beat-up question and then go back and ask for information. The key thing is that you have to determine ahead of time—and prepare your questions accordingly—what mix of information, emotion, and accountability you want.
extracts.chapter: Getting People to Talk: The Art of the Interview

extracts.extract: Sawatsky is one of the few people who has actually studied thousands of interviews to assess what kinds of questions work most of the time, and which ones fail. His basic message is that interviewers should stay in what he calls input mode, asking simple, evocative, open-ended questions that encourage interview subjects to describe and explain, instead of asking aggressive, closed-ended questions that require only that the subject confirm or deny. Sawatsky argues that closed questions, such as 'Were you surprised?' or 'Are you a racist?', while sometimes sounding informed and tough, allow interviewees to answer simply yes or no, then stitch on whatever information they choose.
extracts.chapter: Getting People to Talk: The Art of the Interview

extracts.extract: The most important interviews should be done in person. You can make eye contact and read body language; it is a more human relationship. The best location may be in the person's natural environment, such as an office or home, which may offer evidence of personal or professional interests in the form of mementos, certificates, family pictures, and so on.
extracts.chapter: Getting People to Talk: The Art of the Interview

extracts.extract: A good interview is like a story, with a beginning, a middle, and an end, with high points and other junctures where it is just cruising along. Your strategy probably begins with some sort of icebreaker. Next, you would usually move to factual questions, which will help establish points of agreement with your interviewee. This done, it is time to hike together into more difficult terrain, and tackle contentious questions. It is a lot easier for both parties to do this once they are acquainted. Next, you aim for the peak, presenting your own findings. The key is to get the housekeeping and moderately contentious material out of the way first, in case bringing up an explosive topic prompts your interviewee to suddenly have a pressing appointment.
extracts.chapter: Getting People to Talk: The Art of the Interview

extracts.extract: You can also reduce the chance of prematurely derailing your interview by keeping your questions non-judgmental. Rather than accusing the Wasteglow CEO of illegally burying waste, you should ask incremental, factual questions that get to the point of the illegality without being accusatory. A good beginning might be, 'We have found Document X, which says that Wasteglow buried nuclear waste in the landfill between 2009 and 2014. What do you have to say about this document?' This approach has a greater chance of eliciting an admission than a blunt question.
extracts.chapter: Getting People to Talk: The Art of the Interview

extracts.extract: Silence in an interview is like a vacuum; it's in people's nature to abhor it. The person may fill that awkward silence, and go beyond what they originally intended to say, and even be more honest about it. The pause may draw out the answer your question didn't.
extracts.chapter: Getting People to Talk: The Art of the Interview

extracts.extract: Doing your interviews in the correct sequence is just as important as organizing the flow within each interview. Generally, investigators work from the outside in, advancing through a series of concentric rings until they reach the target of the investigation, at the very centre. You should limit, as much as possible, the chance that word of your activities will reach the centre while you work through the outer rings. The more warning the target has, the easier it is to develop a public-relations strategy to counter your research, or to try to squelch contacts who might speak to you.
extracts.chapter: Getting People to Talk: The Art of the Interview

extracts.extract: In an investigative context, you must record a person's answers in detail. You're not just looking for a few good quotations. You want the context in which the subject says things, the order, the emotional tone of voice, and—often as important as what is said—what is not said. Recording electronically is always the preferred method in any kind of controversial investigation, since it allows you to prove what someone said.
extracts.chapter: Getting People to Talk: The Art of the Interview

extracts.extract: You will want to consider transcribing at least your most important interviews. Transcription alerts you to subtleties that get missed when taking notes. For legally sensitive material—such as when you make allegations about an identified person or corporation—transcription of major interviews is almost mandatory to ensure you have all the points right and are not overlooking something.
extracts.chapter: Getting People to Talk: The Art of the Interview

extracts.extract: Whatever you do in this regard, it is crucial to review each interview after completing it to see how the person's answers have helped supply necessary information and to determine what new questions have been raised.
extracts.chapter: Getting People to Talk: The Art of the Interview

extracts.extract: Avoid using unnamed sources. The public has a right to know who's behind the information and allegations that the media disseminate; this makes sources accountable and lends credibility to what is reported.
extracts.chapter: Getting People to Talk: The Art of the Interview

extracts.extract: If someone absolutely insists on speaking anonymously but you want them on the record, Eric Nalder recommends going back over not-for-attribution portions of an interview, at the time of the interview or later, to see if the subject can be coaxed into letting certain sections be attributed. Once the person has agreed to a few things, you coax more out of them, in a process Nalder calls 'ratcheting.'
extracts.chapter: Getting People to Talk: The Art of the Interview

extracts.extract: The first piece of advice would be to avoid email, whenever possible, for substantive interviews. While it may seem convenient, an emailed Q&A is far too open to manipulation, to prepackaged statements, and to media-relations spin. You cannot see or hear the interviewee. Spontaneity is non-existent, and follow-up questions require another email exchange, allowing a chance for the subject to simply end the discussion.
extracts.chapter: Getting People to Talk: The Art of the Interview

extracts.extract: When it comes to the key accountability interviews, you must speak to the people who are truly accountable. Media officers will try to insist on answering your questions themselves. Make no mistake; this is a power struggle. The media person wants to keep the accountable person out of the spotlight. You need the accountable official because readers, viewers, and listeners aren't stupid. They know the difference in heft between the public-relations officer and the head honcho.
extracts.chapter: Getting People to Talk: The Art of the Interview

extracts.extract: Only by developing a good understanding of the numbers can you find the compelling story. Numbers do lie. So it's important to ask questions, and, when possible, do the math yourself.
extracts.chapter: Following the Money: Seeing the Business Angle in Any Story

extracts.extract: Whenever a story includes a question about how a specific policy, law, or government commitment came to be, one of the places we seek the answer is in the listing of meetings with paid influencers.

There's a great Woody Allen quote about 85 per cent of success is just showing up. I kind of think journalism is a bit the same. Most of the good stories come just from bothering to ask the question or, in this case, bothering to see the document.
We found one of the many people who had been stunned with a Taser and featured him in our pieces through discovery of his online comments in response to a CBC forum on stun guns.
extracts.notes: CP-CBC Taser investigation; finding affected individuals through online comments

extracts.extract: Many RCMP Taser reports were poorly filled out. In fact, in tracing dates of some serious injuries and deaths reported in the media, we found no official reports of these Taser incidents at all. For several months, submitting the form to headquarters had been optional, meaning gaps in the record.
extracts.notes: CP-CBC Taser investigation; data quality issues in official records

extracts.extract: The most satisfying part of the project was bringing statistical and scientific rigour to a much-debated and highly polarized subject: the RCMP's use of Tasers.
Journalists using a court document must be certain that no court order restricts the right to publish its contents.
extracts.chapter: Publication Bans and Other Restrictions on Court Documents

extracts.extract: Use the author search. Google will separate out documents that list your subject as an author. One of the less used—but highly valuable—searches for your checklist is finding everything the subject of your research has written.
